# `@shardworks/cartograph-apparatus`

The Cartograph stands up the **decomposition-ladder** substrate: a typed
data layer for tracking long-lived patron intent across four levels —
**vision** (top, patron-owned, long-lived) → **charge** (first
decomposition, the unit of patron walkthrough) → **piece** (recursive,
internal organization, self-nesting) → **mandate** (the existing leaf
where rigs attach).

This commission lands the **data-and-typed-API substrate plus a
patron-facing CLI**. There is no agent runtime and no downstream
consumer in this package — those land in follow-on commissions. What
ships here:

- Three new writ types contributed to the Clerk: `vision`, `charge`,
  `piece`. Each uses a six-state mandate-clone lifecycle (no
  `childrenBehavior` cascade — patron-walkthrough semantics will be
  coordinated by the typed API and downstream consumers, not by
  registry-side cascade rules).
- Three companion books (`visions`, `charges`, `pieces`) keyed by the
  writ id, each holding a typed companion document with the per-type
  stage and audit fields.
- A `CartographApi` exposed via `provides` that is the **only** layer
  enforcing the ladder's parent invariants:
  - vision has no parent;
  - `charge.parentId` must be a vision;
  - `piece.parentId` must be a charge or piece.
  Raw `clerk.post({ type: 'vision' })` continues to succeed without
  parent-type checks — the typed API is the validator. The mandate-side
  rules (mandate may attach under any non-terminal node) stay where they
  are.
- A patron-facing CLI surface contributed via `supportKit.tools` —
  three subcommand groups (`nsg vision`, `nsg charge`, `nsg piece`),
  each with five operations (`create`, `show`, `list`, `patch`,
  `transition`) plus the on-disk authoring tool `nsg vision apply`.
  The framework `nsg` auto-builder discovers the tools at startup and
  groups them by hyphen prefix; no edits to the framework CLI package
  are required. Every tool routes through the typed API above, so the
  parent invariants and lifecycle coupling hold for CLI-driven
  authoring.

The Cartograph requires `stacks` and `clerk` and recommends `oculus`. The
Oculus writs page automatically renders the new types via the
type-vocabulary helper; without oculus the data is invisible to the
dashboard.

---

## Installation

```json
{
  "dependencies": {
    "@shardworks/cartograph-apparatus": "workspace:*"
  }
}
```

## API

The Cartograph exposes a `CartographApi` via `provides`, accessible at
runtime via `guild().apparatus<CartographApi>('cartograph')`.

```typescript
import type { CartographApi } from '@shardworks/cartograph-apparatus';

const cartograph = guild().apparatus<CartographApi>('cartograph');

// Create a vision (no parent permitted)
const vision = await cartograph.createVision({
  title: 'Land the agentic decomposition ladder',
  body: 'Long-form patron intent ...',
  codex: 'main',
});

// Create a charge under that vision
const charge = await cartograph.createCharge({
  parentId: vision.id,
  title: 'Stand up the data substrate',
  body: 'First decomposition ...',
});

// Create a piece under the charge
const piece = await cartograph.createPiece({
  parentId: charge.id,
  title: 'Pick a companion-doc shape',
  body: 'Internal organization ...',
});

// Pieces can self-nest
const subPiece = await cartograph.createPiece({
  parentId: piece.id,
  title: 'Decide how createX writes both rows atomically',
  body: 'Internal sub-piece ...',
});
```

Each `createX` opens a single `stacks.transaction(...)` and writes the
writ row and the companion doc inside one atomic boundary. Parent
existence, parent-not-terminal, codex inheritance, and id generation
match Clerk's `post()` validation byte-for-byte (the duplication is the
cost of being a typed atomic surface).

### `CartographApi`

```typescript
interface CartographApi {
  // Vision
  createVision(req: CreateVisionRequest): Promise<VisionDoc>;
  showVision(id: string): Promise<VisionDoc>;
  listVisions(filters?: VisionFilters): Promise<VisionDoc[]>;
  patchVision(id: string, fields: Partial<Omit<VisionDoc, 'id'>>): Promise<VisionDoc>;
  transitionVision(id: string, to: { phase: WritPhase; stage: VisionStage; resolution?: string }): Promise<VisionDoc>;

  // Charge
  createCharge(req: CreateChargeRequest): Promise<ChargeDoc>;
  showCharge(id: string): Promise<ChargeDoc>;
  listCharges(filters?: ChargeFilters): Promise<ChargeDoc[]>;
  patchCharge(id: string, fields: Partial<Omit<ChargeDoc, 'id'>>): Promise<ChargeDoc>;
  transitionCharge(id: string, to: { phase: WritPhase; stage: ChargeStage; resolution?: string }): Promise<ChargeDoc>;

  // Piece
  createPiece(req: CreatePieceRequest): Promise<PieceDoc>;
  showPiece(id: string): Promise<PieceDoc>;
  listPieces(filters?: PieceFilters): Promise<PieceDoc[]>;
  patchPiece(id: string, fields: Partial<Omit<PieceDoc, 'id'>>): Promise<PieceDoc>;
  transitionPiece(id: string, to: { phase: WritPhase; stage: PieceStage; resolution?: string }): Promise<PieceDoc>;
}
```

The lifecycle-coupled `transitionX` methods update both `writ.phase` and
the companion doc's `stage` field atomically, inside a single Stacks
transaction. The caller specifies both the target phase and the target
stage explicitly because a single phase may map to multiple stages
depending on context (e.g. a charge moving to `failed` could mean
stage `dropped` or stage `validated` depending on outcome).

### Companion documents

```typescript
interface VisionDoc { id: string; stage: VisionStage; codex?: string; createdAt: string; updatedAt: string; }
interface ChargeDoc { id: string; stage: ChargeStage; codex?: string; createdAt: string; updatedAt: string; }
interface PieceDoc  { id: string; stage: PieceStage;  codex?: string; createdAt: string; updatedAt: string; }

type VisionStage = 'draft' | 'active' | 'sunset' | 'cancelled';
type ChargeStage = 'draft' | 'active' | 'validated' | 'dropped';
type PieceStage  = 'draft' | 'active' | 'done' | 'dropped';
```

The minimal field set is deliberate — the patch surface plus the
`[key: string]: unknown` index signature lets consumers grow the field
set non-breakingly later. Vision text lives on `writ.body`; the
companion doc carries typed metadata only.

### Filters

`VisionFilters`, `ChargeFilters`, and `PieceFilters` mirror the
`PlanFilters` shape from astrolabe — `{ stage?, codex?, limit?, offset? }`.
Lists are ordered by `createdAt desc` (newest first).

## Support Kit

### Books

| Book | Owner | Indexes |
|---|---|---|
| `visions` | `cartograph` | `stage`, `codex`, `createdAt` |
| `charges` | `cartograph` | `stage`, `codex`, `createdAt` |
| `pieces`  | `cartograph` | `stage`, `codex`, `createdAt` |

### Writ Types (contributed to Clerk)

| Name | Description |
|---|---|
| `vision` | Top-level patron intent. No parent. Long-lived. |
| `charge` | First decomposition under a vision; the unit of patron walkthrough. Parent must be a vision. |
| `piece`  | Recursive internal organization. Self-nests. Parent must be a charge or piece. |

Each writ type uses a six-state mandate-clone lifecycle:
`new` (initial) → `open` (active) → `stuck`/`completed`/`failed`/`cancelled`;
no `childrenBehavior` cascade.

## CLI

Cartograph contributes three subcommand groups to `nsg` — one per writ
type — with the same five operations under each:

| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `nsg vision create --title <t> --body <b> [--codex <c>]` | Create a top-level vision (writ at `phase: new`, doc at `stage: draft`). |
| `nsg charge create --parent-id <vision> --title <t> --body <b> [--codex <c>]` | Create a charge under a vision. |
| `nsg piece create --parent-id <charge\|piece> --title <t> --body <b> [--codex <c>]` | Create a piece under a charge or piece (self-nests). |
| `nsg <type> show <id> [--format text\|json]` | Show the companion doc joined with the writ row. Text mode mirrors `nsg writ show`'s lifecycle-aware block; JSON returns `{ ...doc, writ: { ... } }`. |
| `nsg <type> list [--stage <s>] [--codex <c>] [--limit <n>] [--offset <n>] [--format text\|json]` | Tabular list (STAGE \| ID \| CODEX \| TITLE \| CREATED) ordered by `createdAt desc`. |
| `nsg <type> patch <id> --codex <c>` | Patch the companion doc's `codex`. Title and body live on the writ row — edit them via `nsg writ edit`. |
| `nsg <type> transition <id> --phase <writ-phase> --stage <doc-stage> [--resolution <r>]` | Atomically advance both `writ.phase` and the companion doc's `stage` inside one Stacks transaction. Both `--phase` and `--stage` are required because a single phase may map to multiple stages depending on context. |
| `nsg vision apply <slug> [--severity <s>] [--deadline <d>] [--decay <d>]` | Snapshot the on-disk vision at `<GUILD>/vision/<slug>/` into a vision writ + VisionDoc. Idempotent: first apply creates and binds; later applies sync the file's contents into the bound writ. CLI flags override sidecar values for the surveyor priority-hint payload. |

The CLI tools are read or write according to operation: `show`/`list`
declare `permission: 'read'`; `create`/`patch`/`transition`/`apply`
declare `permission: 'write'`. Every tool declares
`callableBy: ['patron']` — they are not exposed to anima or library
callers in this commission. Only `show` and `list` accept the
`--format text|json` flag; write tools return their result directly
and the framework auto-stringifies.

Short-prefix id resolution (via `clerk.resolveId`) works on every
id-bearing flag: `nsg vision show w-mo123` and
`nsg charge create --parent-id w-mo123` both succeed when the prefix
matches a single writ.

## On-disk vision authoring

Visions are prose-heavy and long-lived; the patron-facing path is to
author them in a normal editor on disk and snapshot the directory into
the cartograph with `nsg vision apply <slug>`. The same code path
handles first-time creation and Nth re-import — the binding is durable
across applies, so the file tree is the source of truth for vision
content while the cartograph remains the source of truth for lifecycle
state and ext metadata.

### File layout

```
<GUILD>/
└── vision/
    └── <slug>/
        ├── vision.md              # long-form vision text → writ.body
        └── vision-metadata.yml    # required sidecar
```

The slug must be a single directory name (lowercase letters, digits,
hyphens, or underscores; no path separators, no leading dots, no
`..`). It is fixed at creation time; rename is not supported.

### Sidecar shape

```yaml
# vision-metadata.yml
title: Land the agentic decomposition ladder    # required
stage: draft                                    # required: draft | active

codex: main                                     # optional
severity: high                                  # optional priority hint
deadline: 2026-06-30                            # optional priority hint
decay: slow                                     # optional priority hint
complexity: medium                              # optional priority hint (sidecar-only)
resolution: patron retired the vision           # optional, used on terminal transitions

# Managed by `nsg vision apply` — do not edit by hand.
visionId: w-...                                 # written after first apply
```

| Field | Required | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `title` | yes | Short human-readable title for the vision writ. |
| `stage` | yes | Lifecycle stage. Allowed initial values are `draft` (writ phase `new`) and `active` (writ phase `open`). `sunset` and `cancelled` are rejected on first apply — a vision cannot be born retired; transition to a terminal stage on a later apply. |
| `codex` | no | Target codex name for the vision. |
| `visionId` | no, system-managed | Written by `apply` after first creation. **Do not edit by hand.** Clear the field only to deliberately re-create the vision (the old writ stays in place and becomes orphaned from this directory). |
| `severity` | no | Priority hint surfaced in `writ.ext['surveyor']`. |
| `deadline` | no | Priority hint surfaced in `writ.ext['surveyor']`. |
| `decay` | no | Priority hint surfaced in `writ.ext['surveyor']`. |
| `complexity` | no | Priority hint surfaced in `writ.ext['surveyor']`. Sidecar-only — there is no CLI flag for this field. |
| `resolution` | no | Passed through to `transitionVision` on terminal moves. |

Unknown keys are logged as a warning and ignored (preserves
forward-compatibility while still surfacing typos).

### `visionId` binding

After the first successful `nsg vision apply <slug>`, the tool writes
the new writ's id back into the sidecar as `visionId`. The yaml is
round-tripped via `parseDocument`, so comments and key order survive
the rewrite, and the file is replaced atomically (write-temp,
rename-over) so a process crash cannot lose the binding.

On every subsequent apply the tool resolves the writ via `visionId`,
updates `writ.body` from `vision.md`, and syncs stage/codex changes
through the typed cartograph API. Edits to the writ are **not**
propagated back to the file — the data flow is one-way (file → writ).

### Stale-binding recovery

If the bound writ has been deleted, cancelled, completed, or failed,
the next apply errors cleanly with no partial writes. To recover,
either restore the writ to a non-terminal state, or clear the
`visionId` field in the sidecar to let the next apply re-create the
vision (the orphaned writ stays in place; `nsg writ cancel` it
manually if needed).

### Priority-hint semantics (`ext['surveyor']`)

Each apply writes a payload into `writ.ext['surveyor']` carrying the
fields enumerated above (`severity`, `deadline`, `decay`,
`complexity`). CLI flags override sidecar values for the merged
result:

```sh
# sidecar severity=low; CLI override wins
nsg vision apply my-vision --severity high
```

Fields that neither source provides are omitted from the payload. The
slot is **always** written — even when the merged payload is `{}` — so
its presence on the writ marks it as having been processed by the
apply tool.

The slot's owner — a future surveyor-apparatus plugin that consumes
these hints — does not yet ship. Until it does, the slot is inert; the
key (`surveyor`) and the payload shape are load-bearing precedent for
that future commission.

## What is *not* in this commission

- No vision-keeper agent runtime. `vision-keeper.md` is a placeholder
  stub; the agent runtime ships in a separate commission.
- No per-type tree command. `nsg writ tree --type vision` already
  renders the writ-level shape; the cartograph-aware tree renderer
  (with stage badges per row) is a future commission.
- No pages — the Oculus writs page auto-renders the new types via its
  type-vocabulary helper.
- No link kinds — parent edges flow through `writ.parentId`. Typed link
  kinds are deferred.
- No `childrenBehavior` cascade — patron-walkthrough semantics are
  coordinated by the typed API and downstream consumers.

61	**Plugin tools** are discovered dynamically from **The Instrumentarium** (the `tools` apparatus). At startup, the CLI calls `createGuild()` to boot the runtime, then queries the Instrumentarium for all installed tools that are CLI-callable. Each tool's Zod param schema is auto-converted to Commander flags. This means the plugin tool surface grows automatically as plugins are installed — `nsg --help` always reflects exactly what's available.
62	
63	Tool names are auto-grouped by hyphen prefix — `session-list` and `session-show` become `nsg session list` and `nsg session show`.
64	
65	Two additional commands bypass the tool registry: `nsg consult` and `nsg convene` (interactive sessions with streaming output — not simple tool invocations). These are built into the v1 CLI and will migrate when the Animator and Parlour expose the necessary APIs.
66	
67	### The Apparatus
68	
69	The guild's operational fabric is provided by apparatus — plugins with a start/stop lifecycle that Arbor starts in dependency order. **The Stacks** is the persistence substrate everything else reads from and writes to. **The Scriptorium** manages codexes — bare clones, draft bindings (worktrees), and the seal-and-push lifecycle. **The Clockworks** is the event-driven nervous system: standing orders bind events to relays, and the summon relay dispatches anima sessions in response. **The Surveyor** is the cartograph-decomposition substrate: it surveys cartograph nodes (visions, charges, pieces), produces structural decompositions, and routes each node to whichever kit-contributed surveyor claims its node-type — see [Surveying Cascade](surveying-cascade.md) for the contract. **The Clerk** handles commission intake, converting patron requests into writs and signaling when work is ready to execute. The Fabricator, Spider, Executor, Loom, and Animator then take it from there — covered in the next section.
70	
71	Each of these is a plugin from the default set, not a built-in. The [Standard Guild](#the-standard-guild) section lists them; the sections that follow document each in detail.
72	
73	### Execution, Sessions, and Works
74	
75	When The Clerk signals a writ is ready, **The Spider** spawns a rig and begins driving it: traversing active engines, dispatching those whose upstream work is complete, and extending the rig by querying **The Fabricator** for engine chains that satisfy declared needs. **The Executor** runs each engine — clockwork engines run their code directly; quick engines launch an anima session.
76	
77	An anima session is an AI process running against an MCP server loaded with the role's tools. Before launch, **The Loom** weaves the session context: system prompt, tool instructions, writ context. **The Animator** then starts the process, monitors it, and records the result. The session exits; the output persists. The Clockworks can also trigger sessions directly via the summon relay, bypassing the rig machinery entirely — The Animator handles both paths the same way.
78	
79	Session output is concrete: modified files committed to a git branch, new documents written to disk, structured data passed as engine yields to downstream steps. When a rig completes, any pending git work is merged, and the result is whatever the patron commissioned — a working feature, a fixed bug, a written report. The patron's codexes are updated; the patron can pull, deploy, and use them.

**Plugin tools** are discovered dynamically from **The Instrumentarium** (the `tools` apparatus). At startup, the CLI calls `createGuild()` to boot the runtime, then queries the Instrumentarium for all installed tools that are CLI-callable. Each tool's Zod param schema is auto-converted to Commander flags. This means the plugin tool surface grows automatically as plugins are installed — `nsg --help` always reflects exactly what's available.

Tool names are auto-grouped by hyphen prefix — `session-list` and `session-show` become `nsg session list` and `nsg session show`.

Two additional commands bypass the tool registry: `nsg consult` and `nsg convene` (interactive sessions with streaming output — not simple tool invocations). These are built into the v1 CLI and will migrate when the Animator and Parlour expose the necessary APIs.

### The Apparatus

The guild's operational fabric is provided by apparatus — plugins with a start/stop lifecycle that Arbor starts in dependency order. **The Stacks** is the persistence substrate everything else reads from and writes to. **The Scriptorium** manages codexes — bare clones, draft bindings (worktrees), and the seal-and-push lifecycle. **The Clockworks** is the event-driven nervous system: standing orders bind events to relays, and the summon relay dispatches anima sessions in response. **The Surveyor** is the cartograph-decomposition substrate: it surveys cartograph nodes (visions, charges, pieces), produces structural decompositions, and routes each node to whichever kit-contributed surveyor claims its node-type — see [Surveying Cascade](surveying-cascade.md) for the contract. **The Clerk** handles commission intake, converting patron requests into writs and signaling when work is ready to execute. The Fabricator, Spider, Executor, Loom, and Animator then take it from there — covered in the next section.

Each of these is a plugin from the default set, not a built-in. The [Standard Guild](#the-standard-guild) section lists them; the sections that follow document each in detail.

### Execution, Sessions, and Works

When The Clerk signals a writ is ready, **The Spider** spawns a rig and begins driving it: traversing active engines, dispatching those whose upstream work is complete, and extending the rig by querying **The Fabricator** for engine chains that satisfy declared needs. **The Executor** runs each engine — clockwork engines run their code directly; quick engines launch an anima session.

An anima session is an AI process running against an MCP server loaded with the role's tools. Before launch, **The Loom** weaves the session context: system prompt, tool instructions, writ context. **The Animator** then starts the process, monitors it, and records the result. The session exits; the output persists. The Clockworks can also trigger sessions directly via the summon relay, bypassing the rig machinery entirely — The Animator handles both paths the same way.

Session output is concrete: modified files committed to a git branch, new documents written to disk, structured data passed as engine yields to downstream steps. When a rig completes, any pending git work is merged, and the result is whatever the patron commissioned — a working feature, a fixed bug, a written report. The patron's codexes are updated; the patron can pull, deploy, and use them.

183:The apparatus that surveys the cartograph — the guild's structural map of visions, charges, and pieces. When a node lands on the cartograph, the Surveyor decomposes it: walking its substance, breaking it into the structural pieces an animator or sage can act on, and recording what was found alongside the node. The Surveyor does not judge what to build; it produces the structural inventory that downstream apparatus and animas reason against.
185:The Surveyor is itself a substrate — a kit-contributable surveyor registry. Each kit ships its own surveyor for the cartograph node-types it understands; the substrate routes a node to whichever surveyor claims it and threads the resulting decomposition back into the cartograph. Without a current survey, the guild has structure on the cartograph but no idea what is inside it.
187:The surveyor's decompositions live alongside the cartograph in the guildhall — the cartograph holds the structure, the surveyor's record holds what was found inside each piece. See [The Surveying Cascade](architecture/surveying-cascade.md) for the substrate's contract.

content.text: Now I have enough context to write the artifacts. Let me write the inventory first.

scope.description: Replace the three cartograph companion books (`cartograph/visions`, `cartograph/charges`, `cartograph/pieces`) with a single `writ.ext['cartograph'] = { stage, codex }` sub-slot on the writ row. Rewrite every typed-API method (`createX`/`showX`/`listX`/`patchX`/`transitionX`) to read/write from the ext slot via `clerk.post` + `clerk.transition` + `clerk.setWritExt` composition inside one `stacks.transaction`, preserving every public signature, projection shape, and atomicity guarantee. Drop the `supportKit.books` block. Migrate the in-package tests (`cartograph.test.ts`, `tools.test.ts`, `vision-apply.test.ts`) off direct companion-book access — including the CDC-event assertions, which now target the writs book filtered by `writ.type`. Update the cartograph README and the source-file docstrings inline as concurrent doc updates.
scope.rationale: The brief frames the entire commission around this storage migration; everything in the inventory hangs off it. Not separable from the API/test refactor — the typed-API surface depends on the new storage and the tests have to follow.

scope.description: Update `vision-apply` to wrap `cartograph.createVision` + `clerk.setWritExt(SURVEYOR_PLUGIN_ID, …)` (first apply) and `clerk.edit` + `cartograph.transitionVision` + `clerk.setWritExt(surveyor)` (Nth apply) in an outer `stacks.transaction`, preserving the single-event-per-apply guarantee on the writs-book stream that the substrate observer will subscribe to.
scope.rationale: Without this wrap, vision-apply produces two writs-book CDC events per apply post-cleanup (one for the cartograph create, one for the surveyor stamp). Today the corresponding contract was about the visions book; the substrate observer in Commission C will key off the writs book instead. Brief is silent on vision-apply specifically but the §3.6 single-event guarantee covers it.

scope.description: Update `docs/architecture/surveying-cascade.md` to reflect the post-cleanup substrate shape: rewrite §3.4 (SurveyDoc → `status['surveyor']`/`ext['surveyor']` on the survey writ, no separate book), §3.6 (collapse the three book-event subscriptions to a single writ-type-filtered subscription on the writs book, restate the single-event-per-apply mechanism), and §3.7 (drop `books.surveys` from the substrate-owned list and replace 'Stamps SurveyDoc' with 'Stamps `status['surveyor']`'). Touch up the incidental references at lines 39, 44, 64, 66, 228, 245, 256, 730 for terminology consistency.
scope.rationale: Brief explicitly calls these three sections out as load-bearing and grants permission for incidental cleanup. Required so Commission C (the surveyor substrate) is briefed against the post-cleanup shape, not a stale design.

decisions.question: How should `createX` and `transitionX` achieve atomic writes of `writ.phase` + `writ.ext['cartograph']`?
decisions.context: The brief explicitly prescribes wrapping `clerk.transition` + `clerk.setWritExt` calls in a single `stacks.transaction`. Stacks' `runTransaction` flattens nested transactions so the inner clerk methods reuse the outer tx (stacks-core.ts:191-242). Today's cartograph inlines the writ-row write directly via `tx.book<WritDoc>('clerk', 'writs').put(...)` instead.

options.compose: Outer `stacks.transaction(async () => { await clerk.post(...); await clerk.transition(...); await clerk.setWritExt(...); })` — inner clerk methods flatten into the outer tx; one CDC event per logical operation after coalescing.
options.inline: Open `stacks.transaction(async (tx) => {...})` and write the writ row + ext sub-slot directly via `tx.book<WritDoc>('clerk', 'writs').patch(...)` (replicating setWritExt's read-modify-write inline; replicating clerk.post / clerk.transition validation byte-for-byte as today).

decisions.rationale: Brief-prescribed verbatim ("wrap the clerk.transition and clerk.setWritExt calls in a single stacks.transaction"). Composition is also less duplication — clerk.post / clerk.transition / clerk.setWritExt own their own validation, the cartograph stops re-implementing it. Stacks' flatten-and-coalesce semantics make this safe.

decisions.question: How should `listX` filter on `stage` once the companion books are gone?
decisions.context: The brief says "Filtering on `stage` becomes a JSON-field filter against `writ.ext['cartograph'].stage`; Stacks' query layer supports this." SQLite renders `json_extract(content, '$.ext.cartograph.stage') = ?`; the memory backend uses `getNestedField` for the same dot-notation path. There is no `ext.*` index on the writs book today, so unindexed queries scan the type-narrowed rows.

options.json-path: Cartograph builds a `WhereClause` that includes `['ext.cartograph.stage', '=', stage]` and runs it directly against `stacks.book<WritDoc>('clerk', 'writs')`.
options.in-memory: Cartograph calls `clerk.list({ type: 'vision' })` then filters by stage in memory after the fact.
options.extend-clerk-list: Add ext-keyed filters to `WritFilters` so `clerk.list` accepts them; cartograph routes through `clerk.list`.

decisions.rationale: Brief-prescribed ("Stacks' query layer supports this"). In-memory filtering breaks pagination semantics. Extending `WritFilters` is overreach for a single-consumer need; cartograph already privileged-accesses the writs book elsewhere.

decisions.question: Should the writs book gain an index on `ext.cartograph.stage` for faster cartograph list queries?
decisions.context: Stacks supports JSON-path indexes via SQLite's expression indexes, but `supportKit.books` is per-plugin: each plugin only declares indexes for books it owns. The writs book is owned by Clerk; the cartograph cannot contribute an index to it via its own supportKit. Adding the index would require a Clerk-side change. Without an index, list queries scan rows narrowed by the `[type]` index and run `json_extract` per row — fine at the cartograph's current scale.

options.no-index: Skip; rely on the `[type]` index to narrow candidates; revisit if scale demands it. No cross-package change.
options.add-index: Modify `clerk-apparatus`'s `supportKit.books.writs.indexes` to add `'ext.cartograph.stage'`. Cross-package; out of cartograph's natural surface area.

decisions.rationale: Brief is silent on indexes. The `[type]` index already narrows the scan to the relevant writs. Cross-package index additions are overreach for this commission and produce no observable behavior change at current scale.

decisions.question: Should `ext['cartograph']` carry `codex`, given `writ.codex` already exists as a top-level field on the writ row?
decisions.context: The brief defines `interface CartographExt { stage; codex?; }` explicitly and calls `ext['cartograph']` the canonical home for stage AND codex. But `writ.codex` is a pre-existing top-level field that the cartograph already writes at creation, used elsewhere (codex inheritance during child creation, the writs page filters). Both fields would carry the same value at creation time and could potentially drift via patchX.

options.duplicate: Write codex to both `writ.codex` and `ext['cartograph'].codex` (brief-literal). The projection sources from `ext['cartograph'].codex`; child codex inheritance reads `parent.codex` (the writ row).
options.ext-only: Drop `codex` from `ext['cartograph']`; project from `writ.codex` directly. Closer to DRY.
options.writ-only-on-ext: Drop `writ.codex` for cartograph types; project from `ext['cartograph'].codex` only. Breaks codex-inheritance reads on parents.

decisions.rationale: Brief literal: `CartographExt { stage; codex?; }`. Reading the brief's framing, `ext['cartograph']` is the cartograph's owned, self-contained slot; downstream observers (the substrate's CDC handler) can read all cartograph metadata from one slot without crossing into `writ.codex`. The duplicate is contained — only the cartograph writes both, drift potential is bounded by D5's patch contract.

decisions.question: Where does `patchX(id, { codex })` write the codex value?
decisions.context: Today `vision-patch` / `charge-patch` / `piece-patch` only support patching `codex`. After cleanup, given D4's duplicate-codex choice, the patch could write to `writ.codex` (via `clerk.edit({ codex })`, which restricts changes to `phase === 'new'`), to `ext['cartograph'].codex` (via `setWritExt`, no phase restriction), or both. `clerk.edit`'s `phase === 'new'` restriction would break codex patching on active writs.

options.ext-only: Patch only `ext['cartograph'].codex` via `setWritExt`. The projection (which reads from ext.cartograph.codex per D4) reflects the change immediately. `writ.codex` stays at its creation value.
options.both-when-allowed: Patch `ext['cartograph'].codex` always; also patch `writ.codex` via `clerk.edit` when `phase === 'new'`.
options.writ-only: Patch only `writ.codex` via `clerk.edit` (subject to its `phase === 'new'` restriction). The projection reads writ.codex directly. Breaks if D4 lands as 'duplicate'.

decisions.rationale: Single source of truth for the cartograph's owned slot. `writ.codex` retains its creation-time value as the writ-level codex (used by codex-inheritance for child creation and by the writs page). Drift between `writ.codex` and `ext['cartograph'].codex` post-patch is intentional: writ.codex represents the writ's foundational codex; ext.cartograph.codex represents the current cartograph-projected codex.

decisions.question: What should `showX` / `listX` / projection do when a writ of cartograph type lacks an `ext['cartograph']` slot?
decisions.context: Today `showVision(id)` throws `Vision "X" not found` when the companion doc is missing. After the migration, a vision writ posted via raw `clerk.post({ type: 'vision' })` (bypassing the typed API) would have no `ext.cartograph` slot. The projection needs a defined behavior for that case.

options.fail-loud: Throw with a descriptive error: `Writ "X" has no cartograph metadata (ext.cartograph missing). Cartograph writs must be created via the typed API.`
options.default-fill: Default to `{ stage: 'draft', codex: writ.codex }` — silently fill the missing slot.
options.skip-in-list: In `listX`, silently skip writs missing the slot; in `showX`, throw.

decisions.rationale: Default 2: prefer fail-loud to silent fallback. The cartograph's typed API is the single sanctioned creation path; an unstamped writ of cartograph type is a data-integrity issue that should surface, not silently get a synthesized stage.

decisions.question: Should the cartograph apparatus drop its `supportKit.books` declaration entirely, or retain an empty object?
decisions.context: Today the cartograph contributes `books: { visions: {...}, charges: {...}, pieces: {...} }` via `supportKit.books`. Post-cleanup, none of these books exist. The Stacks book reconciliation creates books on-demand from supportKit declarations — leaving an empty `books: {}` would be inert; omitting `books` entirely is cleaner.
decisions.rationale: Default 1: prefer removal. The brief says "Three Stacks books are removed entirely" — including the supportKit declarations matches the brief's intent. Empty-object preservation has no callers and just adds noise.

decisions.question: How do the test fixtures in `cartograph.test.ts` / `tools.test.ts` / `vision-apply.test.ts` migrate off the companion books?
decisions.context: All three fixtures call `memBackend.ensureBook({ ownerId: 'cartograph', book: 'visions' / 'charges' / 'pieces' }, { indexes: [...] })`. Post-cleanup these calls reference books that don't exist; if left in, they create dead books that nothing reads or writes.

options.remove: Delete the three `ensureBook` calls per fixture. The writs book (clerk-owned) already gets pre-created.
options.keep-defensive: Leave the calls in place — they're harmless and document the historical shape.

decisions.rationale: Default 1: prefer removal. Dead fixture setup is misleading future-readers; the brief explicitly removes the books, the fixtures should follow.

decisions.question: How should `cartograph.test.ts` migrate the assertion 'exactly one CDC event on the cartograph visions book per createVision'?
decisions.context: The current test (cartograph.test.ts:343) calls `stacks.watch<VisionDoc>('cartograph', 'visions', ...)` and counts create/update events. After cleanup, the visions book is gone. The semantically-equivalent assertion targets the writs book and filters by `entry.type === 'vision'`.

options.rewrite-watch: Rewrite the watch to listen on `'clerk', 'writs'` and filter by `entry.type === 'vision'` inside the handler. Assert one event per create.
options.drop-test: Drop the test — coalescing is exercised at the Stacks layer's own conformance suite.

decisions.rationale: The single-event-per-apply guarantee is documented in surveying-cascade.md §3.6 and load-bearing for the substrate. The cartograph-side test is the closest contract enforcement; dropping it would put the contract behind the substrate's later test pass and lose the regression net.

decisions.question: Should `vision-apply` wrap its multi-call sequences in an outer `stacks.transaction` to preserve the single-event-per-apply guarantee on the writs book?
decisions.context: Pre-cleanup vision-apply calls `cartograph.createVision(...)` then `clerk.setWritExt(SURVEYOR_PLUGIN_ID, ...)` sequentially — two separate transactions, two separate CDC events. The original 'single-event-per-apply' contract was about the `cartograph/visions` book (1 event there). After cleanup the substrate observer subscribes to the writs book filtered by writ.type; two separate transactions produce two writs-book events. Wrapping both calls in one outer `stacks.transaction` (relying on transaction flattening + coalescing) collapses to one event per apply. Same applies to the Nth-apply path's `clerk.edit` + `cartograph.transitionVision` + `clerk.setWritExt(surveyor)` sequence.

options.wrap: Open `stacks.transaction(async () => {...})` around the full first-apply (and Nth-apply) call sequence. One coalesced CDC event per apply.
options.no-wrap: Leave the calls sequential; substrate observer dedupes via the short-window mechanism §3.6 mentions defensively.
options.extend-cartograph: Extend `cartograph.createVision` to accept an `extraExt` argument so the surveyor stamp lands inside its transaction.

decisions.rationale: Brief-prescribed semantically: surveying-cascade.md §3.6 calls single-event-per-apply 'a correctness requirement, not an optimization' and the cleanup must preserve it. Wrapping is the smallest change that preserves the guarantee. Defensive dedup is for catching integration bugs cheaply, not as a substitute for the upstream guarantee. Extending cartograph's API for one caller's convenience is overreach.

decisions.question: What is the rewrite shape for `surveying-cascade.md` §3.6's substrate-observer subscription description?
decisions.context: Today §3.6 lists three book-event streams: `book.cartograph.visions.{created,updated}`, `book.cartograph.charges.{created,updated}`, `book.cartograph.pieces.{created,updated}`. Brief prescribes: 'Update to a single subscription on the writs book filtered by `writ.type ∈ {vision, charge, piece}`.' The single-event-per-apply paragraph also shifts mechanism description.

options.single-typed-subscription: Replace the three book-stream bullets with a single bullet: 'a single subscription on the writs book (`book.clerk.writs.{created,updated}`) filtered in the handler by `writ.type ∈ {vision, charge, piece}`.' Restate the single-event-per-apply mechanism as 'cartograph's createX/transitionX primitives are already transactional (writ row + ext.cartograph stamped in one stacks.transaction); vision-apply wraps the cartograph call + the ext.surveyor stamp in one outer transaction; Stacks coalescing collapses the writes to one CDC-significant event per apply.'
options.preserve-three: Keep the three book-stream bullets but add a footnote explaining they map to writ-type-filtered subscriptions on the writs book.

decisions.rationale: Brief-prescribed verbatim. The three-stream framing was an artefact of the per-companion-book era; collapsing to one type-filtered subscription is more accurate and matches the new substrate observer's actual subscription shape.

decisions.question: How wide should the surveying-cascade.md cleanup sweep be?
decisions.context: Brief says §3.4, §3.6, §3.7 are the load-bearing rewrites and 'minor incidental references elsewhere in the doc may need to be touched up for consistency.' The inventory's *Surveying-cascade arch doc references* section enumerates eight incidental references at lines 39, 44, 64, 66, 228, 245, 256, 730 that say 'companion doc' / 'VisionDoc' / 'book.cartograph.visions' etc. in passing.

options.load-bearing-only: Rewrite §3.4, §3.6, §3.7 only; leave incidental references alone for the next reader to update.
options.consistency-sweep: Rewrite the three load-bearing sections plus touch the eight incidental references for terminology consistency. No deeper rewrites.

decisions.rationale: Brief explicitly grants permission for the sweep ('may need to be touched up for consistency'). Leaving incidental references stale would force the next reader to mentally correct them every paragraph; the sweep is mechanical.

decisions.question: Should `createX` delegate to `clerk.post` for parent validation / id generation / timestamp stamping, or continue to inline that work as today?
decisions.context: Today's `createX` opens its own `stacks.transaction` and writes the writ row directly via `tx.book<WritDoc>('clerk', 'writs').put(...)`, replicating clerk.post's parent-validation / codex-inheritance / id-generation / timestamp logic byte-for-byte (cartograph.ts:303-307 comment justifies the duplication: 'Clerk's post does not accept an external transaction context'). But Stacks' transaction-flattening means clerk.post DOES compose cleanly inside an outer transaction — the comment's premise was outdated.

options.delegate: Replace the inlined writ-row write with `await clerk.post({ type, title, body, codex, parentId? })` inside the outer cartograph transaction. Drop the duplicated validation. Cartograph still does its own parent-type validation (vision-only / charge-or-piece-only) before calling clerk.post.
options.inline: Keep inlining clerk.post's logic. Today's pattern.

decisions.rationale: Default 3: extend the API at the right layer. Delegation eliminates the byte-for-byte validation duplication that the existing comment flags as 'the cost of being a typed atomic surface' — the cost is no longer necessary. Cartograph still owns the typed-API parent-type invariants; clerk owns generic post-time validation. Less code, single source of truth for writ creation.

decisions.question: How should `createCharge` / `createPiece` read the parent's codex for inheritance, post-cleanup?
decisions.context: Today: read `parent.codex` from the writ row inside the create transaction. Post-cleanup with D4 ('duplicate' codex), the parent's codex is at `parent.codex` AND `parent.ext['cartograph'].codex`; both are written at create time and stay in sync if neither is patched.

options.writ-codex: Read `parent.codex` (the writ row). Existing pattern; works for any parent type (vision/charge/piece all set writ.codex at creation).
options.ext-codex: Read `parent.ext['cartograph'].codex`. Slightly more uniform with the cartograph's owned slot but requires the parent to have ext.cartograph stamped (always true under typed-API discipline).

decisions.rationale: Existing pattern; less coupling to ext-slot internals; cheaper read; works regardless of D4's outcome.

decisions.question: Should the cartograph stop manually generating timestamps in createX / transitionX / patchX bodies, given clerk.post / clerk.transition / clerk.setWritExt all stamp updatedAt internally?
decisions.context: Today's cartograph generates `now = new Date().toISOString()` and writes it to both writ.updatedAt and doc.updatedAt manually inside its transactions. Post-cleanup the writ.updatedAt is stamped by clerk.transition / clerk.setWritExt automatically; the projection's updatedAt sources from writ.updatedAt directly.

options.delegate-timestamps: Drop manual timestamp stamping; rely on clerk.post / clerk.transition / clerk.setWritExt to stamp writ.updatedAt; project from writ.updatedAt.
options.stamp-manually: Keep generating timestamps in the cartograph and pass them through (where the clerk APIs accept them).

decisions.rationale: The brief's framing — 'createdAt/updatedAt were always duplicates of the writ's own timestamps; they go away' — points at delegation. Less code, single source of truth, no chance of drift between writ-stamped and cartograph-stamped timestamps.

decisions.question: Should the projection types `VisionDoc` / `ChargeDoc` / `PieceDoc` continue to expose `createdAt` and `updatedAt` fields, or drop them?
decisions.context: Brief says 'createdAt / updatedAt were always duplicates of the writ's own timestamps; they go away' (storage). Brief also says 'The existing VisionDoc / ChargeDoc / PieceDoc exported types stay as projection shapes (their definitions can be retained verbatim — the only difference is where the data comes from).' Keeping them on the projection is consistent with 'no external behavioral change.'

options.keep-on-projection: Projection includes `createdAt` and `updatedAt`, sourced from `writ.createdAt` / `writ.updatedAt`. Caller-visible shape unchanged.
options.drop-from-projection: Drop `createdAt` / `updatedAt` from `VisionDoc` / `ChargeDoc` / `PieceDoc`. Callers reading them from the projection break.

decisions.rationale: Brief explicitly says definitions stay verbatim and the only difference is where the data comes from. Tests and CLI tools currently read `doc.createdAt` (e.g. render.ts list rendering); preserving the projection shape is part of the brief's no-external-behavioral-change promise.

decisions.question: Should the brief's `interface CartographExt { stage; codex?; }` shape be exported from the cartograph package as a public type?
decisions.context: Brief defines the shape; downstream consumers (notably the substrate's CDC observer in Commission C) will need to read this slot off `writ.ext['cartograph']` and decode it. Exporting the type lets them reuse the cartograph's definition rather than re-typing it.

options.export: Add `export interface CartographExt { stage: VisionStage | ChargeStage | PieceStage; codex?: string }` to the public type surface (`types.ts` + `index.ts`).
options.internal: Keep the type internal to the cartograph apparatus; downstream consumers inline their own shape declaration.

decisions.rationale: Default 3: extend the API at the right layer. The cartograph owns the slot; exporting its shape is the layered way to share the contract with downstream readers (substrate, future surveyor implementations). Internal-only forces every consumer to duplicate the shape and risks drift.

Body:
  # Cartograph: collapse companion docs into `writ.ext['cartograph']`
  
  ## Intent
  
  The Cartograph apparatus today maintains three companion books — `cartograph/visions`, `cartograph/charges`, `cartograph/pieces` — each holding a `{id, stage, codex, createdAt, updatedAt}` document keyed one-for-one to a writ id. This pattern predates the writ-level `ext` slot. Now that `writ.ext` exists as a sanctioned plugin-keyed metadata slot with a transactional read-modify-write API, those companion books carry pure metadata that can live directly on the writ row.
  
  This commission collapses the three companion books into a single `writ.ext['cartograph'] = { stage, codex }` sub-slot. The cartograph's typed-API surface stays — every existing callable continues to work with the same arguments and return shapes — but the internals shed three Stacks books, three companion-doc shapes, and the dual-write transactional ceremony around them. `createdAt` / `updatedAt` were always duplicates of the writ's own timestamps; they go away.
  
  ## Motivation
  
  - **The companion-doc pattern is the pre-`ext` workaround for plugin-owned writ metadata.** Now that `ext` exists as the sanctioned slot, a pure-metadata companion book is exactly what `ext` was added to replace.
  - **The substrate work that depends on the cartograph (the surveyor-apparatus, see Commission C in the surveying-cascade design subtree) is best designed against the post-cleanup shape.** Building the substrate's CDC observer, completion-stamping logic, and rig routing on top of the current companion-doc pattern, then reshaping it later, is strictly more work than landing the cleanup first. The substrate's CDC observer becomes a writ-type-filtered subscription (a primitive Stacks already supports cleanly) rather than a per-companion-book subscription.
  - **No external behavioral change is intended.** Every consumer (CLI tools, tests, the apply CLI shipped in Commission A) reads the same shape from the same API. The cleanup is internal.
  
  ## Non-negotiable decisions
  
  ### 1. `ext['cartograph']` is the canonical home for stage and codex
  
  ```typescript
  interface CartographExt {
    stage: VisionStage | ChargeStage | PieceStage;  // type depends on the writ type
    codex?: string;
  }
  ```
  
  The plugin id is `cartograph`. The slot is plugin-keyed under the established convention; the cartograph apparatus is the only writer.
  
  ### 2. The typed-API surface stays stable
  
  `CartographApi` keeps every method it has today:
  
  - `createVision` / `createCharge` / `createPiece`
  - `showVision` / `showCharge` / `showPiece`
  - `listVisions` / `listCharges` / `listPieces`
  - `patchVision` / `patchCharge` / `patchPiece`
  - `transitionVision` / `transitionCharge` / `transitionPiece`
  
  Argument shapes (`CreateVisionRequest`, `TransitionChargeRequest`, etc.) and return shapes (`VisionDoc`, `ChargeDoc`, `PieceDoc`) stay. The doc shapes are projections over `writ.ext['cartograph']` rather than rows in a separate book — but from the caller's view, nothing changes.
  
  `createdAt` and `updatedAt` on the projection map to the writ's own timestamps.
  
  ### 3. Atomicity for create + transition
  
  `clerk.transition` silently strips `ext` from its body (per the existing Clerk contract). Any operation that touches both `writ.phase` and `writ.ext['cartograph']` MUST wrap the `clerk.transition` and `clerk.setWritExt` calls in a single `stacks.transaction` so the pair is atomic. This applies to `createX` (post + setWritExt) and `transitionX` (transition + setWritExt) alike.
  
  The existing `createVision({ stage })` parameter — which Commission A relied on for the single-event-per-apply guarantee — keeps its semantic: the cartograph writes the writ at its initial phase, stamps `ext['cartograph']` with the supplied stage, and (if the stage isn't the writ-type's initial state) transitions to it, all inside one `stacks.transaction`.
  
  ### 4. Read-side projection
  
  `showVision(id)` / `listVisions(filters)` continue to return `VisionDoc`-shaped values. Internally they:
  
  - read the writ via `clerk.show(id)` (or `clerk.list({ type: 'vision' })`)
  - project `writ.ext['cartograph'].stage` and `writ.ext['cartograph'].codex` onto the returned shape
  - carry the writ's `createdAt` and `updatedAt` directly
  
  The existing `VisionDoc` / `ChargeDoc` / `PieceDoc` exported types stay as projection shapes (their definitions can be retained verbatim — the only difference is where the data comes from). Filtering on `stage` becomes a JSON-field filter against `writ.ext['cartograph'].stage`; Stacks' query layer supports this.
  
  ### 5. Companion-book deletion
  
  `cartograph/visions`, `cartograph/charges`, and `cartograph/pieces` Stacks books are removed entirely. There is no migration of historical companion-doc rows — the cartograph is recently-shipped and nothing in production-style guild data depends on those books. Any test fixtures or integration tests that opened the companion books directly should be updated to read via the typed API.
  
  ### 6. Surveying-cascade arch doc updates
  
  The settled architecture for the surveying cascade (`docs/architecture/surveying-cascade.md`) names the companion-doc pattern explicitly in three sections. Update them to reflect the post-cleanup shape:
  
  - **§3.4 — Companion `SurveyDoc` holds envelope metadata only.** SurveyDoc was modelled on the cartograph companion-doc pattern. Rewrite this section so the survey-writ envelope metadata (rigVersion, surveyorId, completedAt — minus targetNodeId, which is already `writ.parentId`, and minus rigName, which is already `writ.type`) lives in `status['surveyor']` / `ext['surveyor']` sub-slots on the survey writ rather than in a `books.surveys` companion book. Note that `completedAt` is redundant with `writ.resolvedAt`. The substrate is the only writer.
  
  - **§3.6 — Substrate watches cartograph CDC.** The current text lists three book-event streams (`book.cartograph.visions.{created,updated}`, `book.cartograph.charges.{created,updated}`, `book.cartograph.pieces.{created,updated}`). Update to a single subscription on the writs book filtered by `writ.type ∈ {vision, charge, piece}`. The single-event-per-apply guarantee discussion stays — it shifts from "wrap createVision + transition in one transaction" to "the cartograph's createX/transitionX primitives are already transactional" with the same outcome (one CDC-significant phase transition per apply).
  
  - **§3.7 — Substrate plugin shape.** The substrate-owned-things list includes "Owns survey writ types and `books.surveys`." Drop `books.surveys` from the list. The substrate now owns the `status['surveyor']` and `ext['surveyor']` slots on survey writs instead. Likewise drop "Stamps SurveyDoc on completion" and replace with "Stamps `status['surveyor']` on completion."
  
  These three sections are the only load-bearing references; minor incidental references elsewhere in the doc may need to be touched up for consistency.
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - **Migrating SurveyDoc itself.** The surveyor-apparatus doesn't exist yet (Commission C in the surveying-cascade design subtree). This commission updates the arch doc to reflect the post-cleanup shape so Commission C is briefed against it, but does not build the substrate.
  - **Migrating other companion-doc patterns elsewhere in the framework.** The Astrolabe `PlanDoc` and similar are out of scope; PlanDoc is mostly content (inventory, scope, decisions, spec) and isn't a metadata-only companion.
  - **Reworking the `ext` API itself.** `clerk.setWritExt` and the slot-write contract are existing primitives; this commission consumes them, doesn't extend them.
  - **Renaming `VisionDoc` / `ChargeDoc` / `PieceDoc` projection types.** They keep their names and shapes — only their backing storage moves.
  
  ## References
  
  - Parent design subtree: `c-moji050w` — Cartograph + surveying cascade.
  - Downstream consumer: `c-moji0ggh` — Commission C, the surveyor-apparatus substrate, which depends on this cleanup landing first.
  - The `setWritExt` API contract is documented on `ClerkApi` in `clerk-apparatus`'s public types — that's the load-bearing primitive.

1	/**
2	 * Cartograph — public types.
3	 *
4	 * The Cartograph contributes three writ types to the Clerk —
5	 * `vision`, `charge`, and `piece` — and shadows each one with a
6	 * typed companion document stored in its own book.
7	 *
8	 * Companion-doc convention follows astrolabe's `PlanDoc`: the doc id
9	 * matches the writ id one-for-one; both rows are written under one
10	 * `stacks.transaction(...)` boundary by `createX`. Vision text lives on
11	 * `writ.body`; the companion doc carries typed metadata only — the
12	 * minimal field set is deliberate (D14 in the commission spec).
13	 *
14	 * Stage enums are per-type so each carries the domain-level vocabulary
15	 * relevant to its level of the decomposition ladder (see D10 in the
16	 * commission spec).
17	 */
18	
19	import type { WritPhase } from '@shardworks/clerk-apparatus';
20	
21	// ── Stage enums (per type) ───────────────────────────────────────────
22	
23	/**
24	 * Lifecycle stage on a `VisionDoc`. Captures the patron-facing meaning
25	 * of a vision's progression separately from the writ's `phase`:
26	 *
27	 *  - `draft`     — the vision is being shaped; not yet active.
28	 *  - `active`    — the vision is in flight.
29	 *  - `sunset`    — the patron retired the vision (graceful wind-down).
30	 *  - `cancelled` — the vision was cancelled before reaching `sunset`.
31	 *
32	 * The typed-API `transitionVision` helper is the only sanctioned path
33	 * that updates this field; it writes both `writ.phase` and `stage`
34	 * inside one transaction so the two never drift.
35	 */
36	export type VisionStage = 'draft' | 'active' | 'sunset' | 'cancelled';
37	
38	/**
39	 * Lifecycle stage on a `ChargeDoc`. The `validated` value records the
40	 * patron-walkthrough acceptance moment that distinguishes a charge from
41	 * a piece:
42	 *
43	 *  - `draft`      — the charge is being shaped.
44	 *  - `active`     — the charge is in flight under its parent vision.
45	 *  - `validated`  — the patron walked through and accepted the charge.
46	 *  - `dropped`    — the charge was abandoned without acceptance.
47	 */
48	export type ChargeStage = 'draft' | 'active' | 'validated' | 'dropped';
49	
50	/**
51	 * Lifecycle stage on a `PieceDoc`. Pieces are internal organization;
52	 * the `done` value records completion as a structural fact rather than
53	 * a patron-walkthrough fact:
54	 *
55	 *  - `draft`   — the piece is being shaped.
56	 *  - `active`  — the piece is in flight.
57	 *  - `done`    — the piece is complete.
58	 *  - `dropped` — the piece was abandoned.
59	 */
60	export type PieceStage = 'draft' | 'active' | 'done' | 'dropped';
61	
62	// ── Companion documents ──────────────────────────────────────────────
63	
64	/**
65	 * Companion document for a `vision` writ. Keyed by the writ id one-for-
66	 * one. Carries typed metadata only — vision text lives on `writ.body`.
67	 *
68	 * The `[key: string]: unknown` index signature satisfies Stacks'
69	 * `BookEntry` constraint and lets future commissions grow the field set
70	 * non-breakingly. The patch surface mirrors astrolabe's `PlanDoc.patch`.
71	 */
72	export interface VisionDoc {
73	  /** Index signature required to satisfy BookEntry. */
74	  [key: string]: unknown;
75	  /** The vision writ's id — primary key, matches the writ id. */
76	  id: string;
77	  /** Lifecycle stage. Coupled to `writ.phase` by the typed-API transition helpers. */
78	  stage: VisionStage;
79	  /** Codex this vision targets. Inherited from the writ at creation time. */
80	  codex?: string;
81	  /** ISO timestamp when the doc was created. */
82	  createdAt: string;
83	  /** ISO timestamp of the last mutation. */
84	  updatedAt: string;
85	}
86	
87	/**
88	 * Companion document for a `charge` writ. Same shape rules as
89	 * `VisionDoc` — keyed by writ id, minimal fields, index signature for
90	 * forward compatibility.
91	 */
92	export interface ChargeDoc {
93	  /** Index signature required to satisfy BookEntry. */
94	  [key: string]: unknown;
95	  /** The charge writ's id — primary key, matches the writ id. */
96	  id: string;
97	  /** Lifecycle stage. Coupled to `writ.phase` by the typed-API transition helpers. */
98	  stage: ChargeStage;
99	  /** Codex this charge targets. Inherited from the writ at creation time. */
100	  codex?: string;
101	  /** ISO timestamp when the doc was created. */
102	  createdAt: string;
103	  /** ISO timestamp of the last mutation. */
104	  updatedAt: string;
105	}
106	
107	/**
108	 * Companion document for a `piece` writ. Same shape rules as
109	 * `VisionDoc`/`ChargeDoc` — keyed by writ id, minimal fields, index
110	 * signature for forward compatibility.
111	 */
112	export interface PieceDoc {
113	  /** Index signature required to satisfy BookEntry. */
114	  [key: string]: unknown;
115	  /** The piece writ's id — primary key, matches the writ id. */
116	  id: string;
117	  /** Lifecycle stage. Coupled to `writ.phase` by the typed-API transition helpers. */
118	  stage: PieceStage;
119	  /** Codex this piece targets. Inherited from the writ at creation time. */
120	  codex?: string;
121	  /** ISO timestamp when the doc was created. */
122	  createdAt: string;
123	  /** ISO timestamp of the last mutation. */
124	  updatedAt: string;
125	}
126	
127	// ── Filters ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
128	
129	/**
130	 * Filters for listing visions. Mirrors astrolabe's `PlanFilters` shape
131	 * with `stage` replacing `status`. Lists are ordered by `createdAt desc`.
132	 */
133	export interface VisionFilters {
134	  /** Filter by stage. */
135	  stage?: VisionStage;
136	  /** Filter by codex. */
137	  codex?: string;
138	  /** Maximum number of results (default: 20). */
139	  limit?: number;
140	  /** Number of results to skip. */
141	  offset?: number;
142	}
143	
144	/** Filters for listing charges. Mirrors `VisionFilters`. */
145	export interface ChargeFilters {
146	  /** Filter by stage. */
147	  stage?: ChargeStage;
148	  /** Filter by codex. */
149	  codex?: string;
150	  /** Maximum number of results (default: 20). */
151	  limit?: number;
152	  /** Number of results to skip. */
153	  offset?: number;
154	}
155	
156	/** Filters for listing pieces. Mirrors `VisionFilters`. */
157	export interface PieceFilters {
158	  /** Filter by stage. */
159	  stage?: PieceStage;
160	  /** Filter by codex. */
161	  codex?: string;
162	  /** Maximum number of results (default: 20). */
163	  limit?: number;
164	  /** Number of results to skip. */
165	  offset?: number;
166	}
167	
168	// ── Create requests ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
169	
170	/**
171	 * Request to create a top-level vision. Visions have no parent — passing
172	 * a non-empty `parentId` is rejected by the typed API.
173	 *
174	 * The optional `phase` and `stage` fields let a caller (e.g.
175	 * `nsg vision apply`) bootstrap a vision directly into an active state
176	 * inside the same atomic transaction that creates the writ row and the
177	 * companion VisionDoc. The phase/stage pair must match the fixed mapping
178	 * the typed API maintains for initial creation:
179	 *
180	 *   - phase `'new'`  paired with stage `'draft'`  (default)
181	 *   - phase `'open'` paired with stage `'active'`
182	 *
183	 * Terminal initial states (`sunset` / `cancelled`) are rejected — a vision
184	 * cannot be born retired. To retire a vision, create it then call
185	 * `transitionVision`.
186	 */
187	export interface CreateVisionRequest {
188	  /** Short human-readable title describing the vision. */
189	  title: string;
190	  /** Long-form vision text, stored on `writ.body`. */
191	  body: string;
192	  /** Optional target codex. */
193	  codex?: string;
194	  /**
195	   * Optional initial phase on the underlying writ. Defaults to `'new'`.
196	   * Must be paired with a matching `stage` per the initial-state mapping.
197	   */
198	  phase?: WritPhase;
199	  /**
200	   * Optional initial stage on the companion VisionDoc. Defaults to
201	   * `'draft'`. Must pair with `phase` per the initial-state mapping.
202	   * Terminal stages (`sunset`, `cancelled`) are rejected.
203	   */
204	  stage?: VisionStage;
205	}
206	
207	/**
208	 * Request to create a charge under a vision. The typed API rejects when
209	 * `parentId` is missing or when the parent's writ type is not `vision`.
210	 */
211	export interface CreateChargeRequest {
212	  /** Required: must reference an existing vision in a non-terminal state. */
213	  parentId: string;
214	  /** Short human-readable title describing the charge. */
215	  title: string;
216	  /** Detail text, stored on `writ.body`. */
217	  body: string;
218	  /** Optional target codex. Defaults to the parent vision's codex. */
219	  codex?: string;
220	}
221	
222	/**
223	 * Request to create a piece under a charge or piece. The typed API
224	 * rejects when `parentId` is missing or when the parent's writ type is
225	 * not `charge` or `piece`.
226	 */
227	export interface CreatePieceRequest {
228	  /** Required: must reference an existing charge or piece in a non-terminal state. */
229	  parentId: string;
230	  /** Short human-readable title describing the piece. */
231	  title: string;
232	  /** Detail text, stored on `writ.body`. */
233	  body: string;
234	  /** Optional target codex. Defaults to the parent's codex. */
235	  codex?: string;
236	}
237	
238	// ── Transition requests ──────────────────────────────────────────────
239	
240	/**
241	 * Lifecycle-coupled transition request for a vision. The caller supplies
242	 * both the target `phase` and the target `stage` because the mapping is
243	 * not always one-to-one — for example, a vision driven to `failed` might
244	 * mean stage `cancelled` or stage `sunset` depending on context. The
245	 * typed API writes both fields atomically.
246	 */
247	export interface TransitionVisionRequest {
248	  /** Target phase on the underlying writ. */
249	  phase: WritPhase;
250	  /** Target stage on the companion VisionDoc. */
251	  stage: VisionStage;
252	  /** Optional resolution string. Set on terminal transitions. */
253	  resolution?: string;
254	}
255	
256	/**
257	 * Lifecycle-coupled transition request for a charge. See
258	 * `TransitionVisionRequest` for why both `phase` and `stage` are
259	 * specified explicitly.
260	 */
261	export interface TransitionChargeRequest {
262	  /** Target phase on the underlying writ. */
263	  phase: WritPhase;
264	  /** Target stage on the companion ChargeDoc. */
265	  stage: ChargeStage;
266	  /** Optional resolution string. Set on terminal transitions. */
267	  resolution?: string;
268	}
269	
270	/**
271	 * Lifecycle-coupled transition request for a piece. See
272	 * `TransitionVisionRequest` for why both `phase` and `stage` are
273	 * specified explicitly.
274	 */
275	export interface TransitionPieceRequest {
276	  /** Target phase on the underlying writ. */
277	  phase: WritPhase;
278	  /** Target stage on the companion PieceDoc. */
279	  stage: PieceStage;
280	  /** Optional resolution string. Set on terminal transitions. */
281	  resolution?: string;
282	}
283	
284	// ── API ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
285	
286	/**
287	 * The `provides` interface for the Cartograph apparatus.
288	 *
289	 * The typed-API surface is the **only** layer that enforces the ladder's
290	 * parent invariants:
291	 *
292	 *   - `createVision` rejects any non-empty `parentId`.
293	 *   - `createCharge` rejects unless the parent's writ type is `vision`.
294	 *   - `createPiece`  rejects unless the parent's writ type is `charge` or `piece`.
295	 *
296	 * Raw `clerk.post({ type: 'vision' })` continues to succeed without
297	 * parent-type checks — the typed API is the validator. WritTypeConfig
298	 * deliberately carries no parentTypes/allowedChildren restrictions.
299	 *
300	 * Each `createX` opens a single Stacks transaction and writes both the
301	 * writ row and the companion document inside one boundary. Parent
302	 * existence, parent-not-terminal, codex inheritance, and id generation
303	 * mirror Clerk's `post()` validation byte-for-byte (the duplication is
304	 * the cost of being a typed atomic surface).
305	 *
306	 * Each `transitionX` writes both `writ.phase` and the companion doc's
307	 * `stage` field atomically. The caller specifies both targets
308	 * explicitly because a single phase may map to multiple stages
309	 * depending on context.
310	 */

312	  // ── Vision ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
313	
314	  /** Create a top-level vision. Rejects when `parentId` is set. */
315	  createVision(request: CreateVisionRequest): Promise<VisionDoc>;
316	  /** Show a vision by writ id. Throws if the doc is not found. */
317	  showVision(id: string): Promise<VisionDoc>;
318	  /** List visions, ordered by createdAt descending. */
319	  listVisions(filters?: VisionFilters): Promise<VisionDoc[]>;
320	  /** Patch a vision's companion doc. Returns the updated document. */
321	  patchVision(id: string, fields: Partial<Omit<VisionDoc, 'id'>>): Promise<VisionDoc>;
322	  /** Atomically transition both the writ phase and the companion stage. */
323	  transitionVision(id: string, request: TransitionVisionRequest): Promise<VisionDoc>;
324	
325	  // ── Charge ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
326	
327	  /** Create a charge under a vision. Rejects when the parent is not a vision. */
328	  createCharge(request: CreateChargeRequest): Promise<ChargeDoc>;
329	  /** Show a charge by writ id. Throws if the doc is not found. */
330	  showCharge(id: string): Promise<ChargeDoc>;
331	  /** List charges, ordered by createdAt descending. */
332	  listCharges(filters?: ChargeFilters): Promise<ChargeDoc[]>;
333	  /** Patch a charge's companion doc. Returns the updated document. */
334	  patchCharge(id: string, fields: Partial<Omit<ChargeDoc, 'id'>>): Promise<ChargeDoc>;
335	  /** Atomically transition both the writ phase and the companion stage. */
336	  transitionCharge(id: string, request: TransitionChargeRequest): Promise<ChargeDoc>;
337	
338	  // ── Piece ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
339	
340	  /** Create a piece under a charge or piece. Rejects on any other parent type. */
341	  createPiece(request: CreatePieceRequest): Promise<PieceDoc>;
342	  /** Show a piece by writ id. Throws if the doc is not found. */
343	  showPiece(id: string): Promise<PieceDoc>;
344	  /** List pieces, ordered by createdAt descending. */
345	  listPieces(filters?: PieceFilters): Promise<PieceDoc[]>;
346	  /** Patch a piece's companion doc. Returns the updated document. */
347	  patchPiece(id: string, fields: Partial<Omit<PieceDoc, 'id'>>): Promise<PieceDoc>;
348	  /** Atomically transition both the writ phase and the companion stage. */
349	  transitionPiece(id: string, request: TransitionPieceRequest): Promise<PieceDoc>;
350	}

Carries priority hints (severity, deadline, decay, complexity) on cartograph nodes — set by the patron via apply CLI for visions, set by the upstream surveyor rig for charges and pieces. The substrate translates these into Reckoner dimensions when emitting the survey petition. |

```

197	<oldChargeId>` link. The old charge stays in the books with its
198	content intact, marked superseded.

201	|---|---|

203	| `cartograph.createCharge({ ..., supersedes })` API supports the link atomically | What threshold of vision-change triggers supersede |
204	| Re-survey rigs receive existing children as input context | How aggressive to be (rewrite vs conserve) |
205	| Superseded charges remain queryable; their dispatched work continues | Whether superseding cascades to children — substrate default: no auto-cascade |
206	| The survey writ records the rig's decision in its notes | Stop conditions, reasoning approach |
207
